When the correct answer is a compound rule statement joined by 'or' or a multi-condition list, ensure each distractor has comparable grammatical complexity and word count so that answer format does not reveal correctness.

Explanations should state the operational requirement directly: name the rule, the applicable categories, the distance or condition, and when it applies. Avoid explanations that merely echo the answer text.

Before generating a new activity, check whether an existing question already tests the identical retrieval target in the identical multiple-choice mode. If so, either skip the activity or design it for a different retrieval mode (e.g., true/false, scenario application, or fill-in-the-blank).
Minima flashcards work well with context on the front and the bare value on the back. Do not flag thin backs on such cards as defects.
Consider generating items for the two uncovered departure minima: 1,500 feet when only Category I aircraft are involved, and 3,000 feet when either is Category II, both from 3-12-3(b).
shard_overviews: This shard covers a single short paragraph with two source blocks that establish two core concepts: the permissible basis for airport traffic control service, and pilot collision-avoidance responsibility alongside the scope of traffic information usefulness. The items generally test the right operational substance and provide good cross-format coverage of both concepts. Four items need intervention: two carry paragraph-location scaffolding, one has a conspicuous answer-length cue, and one has a thin explanation referencing a paragraph number. No items are wrong, educationally harmful, or overloaded.

This shard covers paragraph 3-1-10, a concise rule with one body directive, one phraseology format, and three illustrative examples. Most of the 15 items are correct and educationally sound, testing the two essential concepts (trigger conditions for abnormality advisories and the standard phraseology format) across multiple learning formats. Three interventions are needed: two paragraph-location scaffold repairs and one replacement of a structurally weak fill-in-the-blank item.

Paragraph 3-1-11 (Surface Area Restrictions) has good source fidelity across all 16 items. The core rules — surface-area crossing and speed-limit approval, the 250-knot cap with higher-minimum-speed exception, unusual-maneuver prohibition, and the LOA-based exception — are all represented. No factual errors were found. The situation_action activity is the standout item with realistic operational context and a peer-pressure distractor. However, document-structure scaffolding appears in multiple items (references to 'the paragraph,' 'the note,' 'note-based situations'), answer-length cues are present in two multiple-choice items, and two activities duplicate existing questions in the same retrieval mode. The thin-back flashcard and non-self-contained procedure card reduce retrieval effectiveness.

Shard covers one paragraph (3-1-12) with two simple source statements. Ten of fifteen items are educationally sound and require no intervention. The main defects are paragraph-location scaffolding in three items and one near-duplicate activity pair. One flashcard invents operational reasoning absent from the source blocks.

Shard covers one paragraph (3-1-13) with adequate cross-format coverage of the two conditional rules and prescribed phraseology. Five items need replacement: one fill-blank collapsed to a single choice, two thin true/false items, one near-duplicate multiple-choice activity, and one flashcard front bearing a paragraph-number scaffold. The remaining 11 items are correct, self-contained, and educationally coherent.

The shard covers a single paragraph (3-1-14) with limited source material — two directives qualified by 'to the extent possible' and an explanatory note. Most items are factually accurate and self-contained. Three items require minor remediation: one vacuous true/false question whose stem gives away the answer, one activity with a paragraph-location explanation, and one flashcard with a paragraph-location front.

This single-paragraph shard (3-1-15) is in reasonable shape. The source material is thin — one body block, one note, three phraseology variants, and a reference — and coverage is appropriately focused on the two-part controller response and its phraseology. The most significant recurring defect is answer-length cues on the two items that ask for the full compound rule as a single correct answer; the substantially longer correct choice is distinguishable from the shorter distractors. Thin explanations that restate the answer rather than teaching the operational trigger affect five items across questions and activities. One activity duplicates another activity's content with only a type label change and a paragraph-location explanation. The five flashcards are clean, accurate, and well-scoped. The one scenario-based activity is well-constructed.

All 15 items across questions, activities, and flashcards are source-faithful to paragraph 3-1-2, educationally coherent, and format-appropriate. The shard covers the paragraph's four distinct concepts (LOA requirement, intervention-only-when-needed trigger, elimination of routine approvals, DoD directive equivalence) with cross-format reinforcement. No items required replacement, removal, or splitting.

The shard is solid on source fidelity — all items faithfully reflect paragraph 3-1-3 requirements and no item teaches incorrect ATC substance. Two questions have format or answer-defensibility defects that prevent reliable assessment. The five activities span four distinct activity types (conditional_rule_check, directive_check, knowledge_check, phraseology_builder, requirement_check), providing good format diversity. The seven flashcards are clean and concise — each targets a single retrieval objective, and the thin_back flag on one card is a natural consequence of a source statement that is itself a single sentence.

Chapter 3 shard 10 (paragraph 3-1-4) is a clean, well-formed packet. All 14 items faithfully represent the source material with correct answers, plausible distractors, and appropriate format assignments. One activity explanation uses meta-commentary about the paragraph structure instead of directly teaching the operational requirement — a thin but correctable defect.

This shard covers paragraph 3-1-5 (VEHICLES/EQUIPMENT/PERSONNEL NEAR/ON RUNWAYS) with three core operational concepts: LOA authorization for RSA-edge operations, PROCEED AS REQUESTED phraseology and its runway restriction, and the runway-clear requirement for departures and arrivals. The items are overwhelmingly source-faithful and educationally coherent. Cross-format reinforcement is strong — all three concepts appear in at least two formats. The situation_action activity provides the richest operational practice. Four items need minor intervention: one flashcard front contains paragraph-location scaffolding, and three explanations are too thin to teach the controlling rule. No items are wrong, educationally harmful, or overloaded.

Chapter 3 paragraph 3-1-6 (Traffic Information) is a compact section. The shard's items cover the core rules—vehicle/equipment description for pilot recognition, easy-to-understand relative position, and CTRD phraseology—across all three formats. Quality is mixed: two of five questions are educationally weak (trivia fill-in-the-blank, paragraph-location scaffolding with answer-length cue), two of five flashcards carry explicit paragraph-number references in their fronts, and one activity is a near-exact duplicate of a question. The remaining items are accurate, self-contained, and operationally focused. The phraseology_builder and situation_action activities are strong examples of applied learning.

This shard covers paragraph 3-1-7 (Position Determination), a short directive with two source blocks — one body block stating the prerequisite rule and one note describing preferred and fallback methods. The generated items are generally accurate and operationally sound. All correct answers align with the source. Distractors are plausible and represent operational mistakes. The two true/false questions provide straightforward coverage, and the three scenario-based items (one question, one activity situation_action) effectively test application of the fallback rule. No items are wrong or harmful. The primary defects are two true/false items with vacuous explanations that restate the answer instead of teaching the rule, and two flashcards whose fronts reference paragraph number 3-1-7 by name, substituting document-structure trivia for operational substance.

The shard covers paragraph 3-1-8 (Low Level Wind Shear/Microburst Advisories) with 19 items across three formats. Source fidelity is strong — no item contains factual errors. The primary defects are superficial retrieval design, redundant concept coverage, and thin flashcard context. Five replacements target three near-duplicate items testing the same core distribution rule (four items across three formats ask 'who receives alerts'), one trivial fill-in-blank, one awkward activity instruction, and one context-light flashcard. After remediation, coverage draws from six distinct source concepts instead of recycling the same header rule.

Good. Six of seventeen items required replacement, all for thin explanations or answer-length cues. No source-fidelity errors, no wrong answers, and no educationally harmful items. Flashcards are uniformly clean and well-formed. The chapter paragraph on Use of Tower Radar Displays is well-represented across all three formats with reasonable cross-format reinforcement of the core uncertified/certified distinction.

Paragraph 3-2-1 is a single-sentence body paragraph directing use of ATC light signals from TBL 3-2-1 when radio communications cannot be employed. The shard's items adequately cover the controlling condition (when), scope (who), and individual signal meanings. Principal defects are paragraph/table-location scaffolding embedded in stems and thin explanations that restate answers instead of teaching the controlling rule. Two items teach pure document-structure trivia (which table to consult) and must be removed. Cross-format reinforcement exists between questions cdd2ce47/activity 922b2f06 and question dbbc7ced/activity 36ddc82a, which is appropriate. The table-lookup activity and table-signal flashcards correctly test operational knowledge of signal meanings, though the packet source blocks do not contain the table contents—the paragraph explicitly incorporates TBL 3-2-1 by reference.

The shard contains generally sound items that cover the three triggering conditions, the non-prohibitive nature of the warning signal, and its recipients. The primary defect is cross-paragraph content leakage: three items (one question, one activity, one flashcard) ask what the alternating red-and-green signal means and answer with 'exercise extreme caution,' which is defined in TBL 3-2-1, not in the 3-2-2 source blocks provided in this shard. One other question uses document-structure scaffolding ('note-based') in its stem. The remaining ten items are source-faithful and educationally appropriate.

This shard covers paragraph 3-2-3 receiver-only acknowledgment procedures and Table 3-2-1 ATC light signals. The flashcard set is the strongest format here: all seven cards are clean, correctly keyed to the procedure/table structure, and provide appropriate single-target retrieval. The questions and activities, however, suffer from recurring thin explanations that merely restate the answer without teaching the classification structures (aircraft-type/time-of-day/operating-state for acknowledgments; color-flash/recipient-category for light signals). Two exact or near-duplicate items waste coverage on already-tested concepts. Stem paragraph-location scaffolding in three items references table numbers instead of using descriptive names. No factual errors were found in any item.

The shard covers paragraph 3-3-1 (Landing Area Condition) with 20 items across three formats. Source fidelity is consistently high, and distractor quality is generally strong with plausible operational mistakes. Three items exhibit specific defects: one question has an answer-length cue from unbalanced distractors, one knowledge_check activity is redundant with a question in the same retrieval mode, and one flashcard has a front/back scope mismatch. Four source statements from 3-3-1 remain untested across all formats.

This shard is well-constructed. Items faithfully represent the 3-3-2 closed/unsafe runway procedure across all three formats. The three-step procedural sequence (initial notification, NOTAM quotation with no-clearance statement, and at-own-risk advisory) is covered in correct order, and the ILS exception from note c is also addressed. Source fidelity is strong; all correct answers align with the source blocks. Distractors are operationally plausible. One flashcard is context-light — its front omits the closed/unsafe runway premise, making it not fully self-contained. Three automated negative_stem flags are false positives; none of the flagged items actually use negative stems.

This shard covers a single well-defined operational topic — timely airport condition information under 3-3-3 — with solid source coverage across three formats. Questions and activities use plausible operational distractors and test rule application rather than rote recall. Flashcards are well-formed, focused on single retrieval targets, and valid in both directions. Two items have explanation-quality defects: one uses bare paragraph-location scaffolding, and the other provides a thin explanation that merely restates the question without teaching the operational significance supported by the note block.

The shard covers paragraph 3-3-4 (Braking Action) with 20 items across three formats. Source fidelity is high — all answers align with the controlling text. The primary defects are thin explanations that echo the answer rather than stating the operational directive, and one knowledge_check activity that adds no retrieval diversity beyond the corresponding multiple-choice question. The flashcards are well-structured with appropriately focused retrieval targets, and the phraseology_builder correctly derives from a published source example.

This shard covers two paragraphs (3-3-5 Braking Action Advisories, 3-3-6 Arresting System Operation) with 17 items across all three formats. The underlying operational content is accurate, but every item exhibits paragraph-location scaffolding in either the stem or explanation. Two fill-in-the-blank questions reduce operational knowledge to single-word recall from examples (a runway number and the word RUNWAY). One knowledge-check activity has a pronounced answer-length cue. One flashcard is overloaded with four distinct procedural actions. The phraseology-builder and spot-the-error activities are well-constructed and require no substantive change beyond de-scaffolding their explanations.

Shard 24 covers two paragraphs (3-3-7 FFM Remote Status Unit and 3-4-1 Emergency Lighting) with 21 items across three formats. Source fidelity is generally sound—no factually wrong items were found—but paragraph-location scaffolding is pervasive, especially in 3-3-7 items where every question and most activities reference paragraph numbers in stems or explanations. The 3-4-1 items are stronger overall: stems are mostly self-contained and scenario-based, and explanations are more operational. Two flashcards in 3-4-1 teach paragraph-number trivia rather than rule content. One 3-4-1 question has a structural answer-length cue that reveals the correct choice. No items required removal or splitting; all defects are remediable through targeted replacement.

This shard covers a single paragraph (3-4-10, Runway Edge Lights) with broad format coverage across 19 items. Source fidelity is strong — no factual errors were found. The operational content (timing windows, visibility trigger, NOTAM restriction, scope of the closure exception) is appropriate for a mix of rote recall and rule application. Five items need intervention: one answer-length cue, one duplicate across formats, one thin explanation, one paragraph-location scaffold in a stem, and one flashcard with a single-word back.

Shard 26 covers paragraph 3-4-11 (HIRL/RCLS/TDZL intensity settings) with 20 items across three formats. Source fidelity is sound — all table-lookup answers are correct and no unsafe operational implications exist. The paragraph has two educational elements: the general operating rule (follow the table with three exceptions) and the eight visibility-to-step mappings. Cross-format coverage exists for both elements. The main defect is over-redundancy: the night 3-5 miles → step 2 mapping appears in four items, the exceptions concept in five, and one activity is a near word-for-word duplicate of a question. Two questions have answer-length cues where the correct choice is substantially longer than distractors. Five items render the source text 'and/or' as 'and or'. Explanations for table-lookup items restate the mapping, which is adequate given that the mapping itself is the operational principle.

Shard quality is generally high. Most questions, activities, and flashcards are accurate, well-distracted, and cover the paragraph's three distinct knowledge targets (main operating rule, step-down technique, and TBL 3-4-9 visibility-to-step mappings). Six items required replacement: three for a source-fidelity error in the night Step 1 visibility band, two for answer-length cues, and one for a paragraph-location explanation.

This shard covers a single short paragraph (3-4-13) with one sentence of operational content. The 15 generated items substantially over-test the narrow source material, with several questions, activities, and flashcards targeting the same retrieval concepts. The most common defect is paragraph-location scaffolding embedded in stems, flashcard fronts, and explanations. One activity invents an unsupported rationale in its explanation.

This shard covers a single operational rule (MIRL operation per TBL 3-4-10 with two exceptions) and a six-cell visibility-to-step lookup table. The content is source-faithful with no unsafe operational errors. Most automated flags are heuristic false positives — thin_back for table-lookup flashcards is the expected format, and table/source-label references are citations of authority, not paragraph-location scaffolding. Three items require replacement for distractor quality and explanation depth; two activities duplicate questions and are removed. The remaining 13 items are sound.

This shard covers one concise operational rule (high speed turnoff lights per 3-4-15) with three sub-rules: base operation tied to arrival runway-light use, two shutoff endpoints, and two exceptions (facility directives, pilot request). The content is source-faithful with no unsafe operational errors. Defects are concentrated in explanation quality — three activities and one question have explanations that either reference paragraph numbers or are too thin to teach the controlling principle. One question has an answer-length cue from distractors that are 30-50% shorter than the correct answer. One flashcard has a back consisting solely of 'No' without teaching content. Six items require replacement; the remaining eleven are sound.

This shard covers taxiway-light operating rules and three table types (one-step, three-step, five-step) from paragraph 3-4-16. Content is technically accurate across all item formats. The table-lookup flashcards and activities are well-structured for the source. The main defects are answer-length cues in multiple-choice items where the correct answer is substantially longer than distractors, explanations that restate the answer or cite paragraph numbers instead of teaching the operational rule, and one redundant true/false question that duplicates a multiple-choice item on the same fact.

This shard covers a single sentence from paragraph 3-4-17, a conditional duty with a defined time period. The 12 generated items massively over-cover this one sentence, producing extreme redundancy—half the items are verbatim restatements or near-duplicates of the same two facts (controls condition, sunset-to-sunrise timing). Two flashcards embed paragraph-location trivia ('3-4-17') in their fronts. Three items have thin explanations that merely restate the answer, one true/false question and two true/false activities test verbatim source recognition rather than operational application, and one activity explanation cites the paragraph number. The situation-action activity (cbf90c8a) is the strongest item, placing the rule in a realistic operational scenario with conflicting advice. The remaining clean items—the first multiple-choice question, the knowledge-check activity, and two flashcards—are sound individually but overlap substantially with each other.

This shard covers a single brief paragraph (3-4-18) on rotating beacon use. Most items are factually correct and have plausible distractors. The main defects are paragraph-location scaffolding in stems and explanations, answer-length cues, and flashcard backs that are too thin to stand as retrieval targets. No items require removal or splitting.

This shard covers paragraph 3-4-19 (RWSL) with 20 items across three formats. Source fidelity is high — all answers and distractors are accurate per the source block. The primary defect is thin explanations that restate the answer rather than teaching the controlling rule or operational principle. Three items cite paragraph numbers in explanations. Two flashcards have backs that are too thin to function as standalone retrieval targets. One question exhibits answer-length cueing. No items require removal or splitting. Cross-format coverage is adequate — each major provision in the source has at least one question, activity, and flashcard.

All 17 items in this shard are source-faithful, educationally coherent, and appropriate for their formats. No replacements, removals, or splits are required. The paragraph covers REIL operating rules, turn-off triggers, and intensity-table lookups — all of which are represented across questions, activities, and flashcards with reasonable cross-format coverage.

The shard covers paragraph 3-4-3 (VASI systems) with 18 items. Overall quality is moderate: the scenario-based question and situation_action activity are strong, and flashcards correctly target individual table values. The primary defect is concept clustering — the three-step VASI twilight setting is tested by three distinct items (two questions, one activity) while the two-step system and cross-table distinctions are underrepresented. Three activities need replacement for answer-length cue, type mismatch, and content duplication respectively. One activity is a near-identical binary duplicate of a true_false question and is removed.

This shard covers paragraph 3-4-4 (PAPI) with 17 items across three formats. Questions are generally well-constructed with operationally meaningful stems and plausible distractors. Flashcards are lean but accurate for single-value retrieval targets. Two activities need intervention: one has a misleading question design that collapses three valid pilot-request steps into a single-choice format, and another uses paragraph-location scaffolding in its explanation instead of teaching the controlling principle. One activity is a near-duplicate of an existing question testing the same fact in the same true/false format.

Paragraph 3-4-5 is a procedural rule with a clear structure: two time-of-day regimes, three daytime sub-situations, and two discretionary triggers. The shard's items are generally accurate and source-faithful. The main defects are answer-length cues in two items where the correct choice recites the full regulatory condition while distractors are short fragments, paragraph-location references embedded in stems and explanations, a thin explanation that restates rather than teaches, and one activity that is a near-verbatim duplicate of a question in the same True/False retrieval mode. All seven flashcards are sound. The scenario-based question and situation-action activity are the strongest items — they test application of the disjunctive daytime trigger in realistic operational contexts.

This shard covers ALS intensity settings (3-4-6) with generally accurate items. The table values are well-represented across questions, activities, and flashcards. Exception coverage is solid for facility directives and the night pilot-request limitation. Two near-duplicates waste retrieval diversity — one question repeats the same night-band lookup, and one activity mirrors the normal-rule question. The daylight steps 2/3 recommended-settings concept from the note is entirely untested. Automated flags on this shard are overwhelmingly false positives: stems are positive constructions, and explanations correctly cite TBL 3-4-5 rather than paragraph numbers. The thin_back flags on table-lookup flashcards are benign — the step number is the exact retrieval target.

Paragraph 3-4-7 (Sequenced Flashing Lights) is a short, rule-dense paragraph with three clear operational triggers and one system constraint. All 15 items are source-faithful — none teaches incorrect procedure. The main defects are structural: two items have answer-length cues where the correct choice is a full sentence amid short fragments; two activities are near-duplicates of a question testing the same concept in the same retrieval mode; one activity contains a paragraph-number reference that substitutes document trivia for operational skill; and two flashcards have bare one-word backs that fail to be self-contained retrieval targets. The five kept questions and two kept flashcards are solid across engagement modes.

Paragraph 3-4-8 (MALSR/ODALS) covers two visibility tables with distinct part-time-tower closure rules and three exceptions to the normal table settings. All 19 items are source-faithful — none teaches an incorrect procedure or table value. The main defects are structural: two items (one question, one knowledge-check activity) have answer-length cues where the correct choice is a compound sentence amid short fragments; two true/false activities are near-duplicates of questions testing the same concept in the same retrieval mode; and one flashcard fragments the same exception content already covered by a more comprehensive flashcard. The kept items cover the two-step low-intensity rule, three-step radio-link and fallback-setting rules, table value lookups across both day and night, and the operational scenario for tower-closed setting-2 decisions across formats.

Paragraph 3-4-9 is a short, two-rule paragraph (ALSF-2 trigger + operating modes; SSALR fallback). All 15 generated items are factually correct against the source but suffer from concentrated redundancy — roughly two-thirds of items test the SSALR-when-trigger-inactive concept while the two ALSF-2 operating modes (pilot request, controller judgment) are underrepresented. Answer-length cues, mischaracterized flashcard types, paragraph-location scaffolding, and a thin card back are the main presentation defects.

This shard covers the four runway selection rules of paragraph 3-5-1 with generally high source fidelity and educational coherence. Of 19 items across three formats, 14 are correct as written. The question and activity distractors are mostly plausible operational mistakes. The situation_action activity is a strong application item that requires balancing wind priority against delay pressure. The main defects are thin explanations that restate the answer without teaching the complete rule including its exceptions, one flashcard with an incomplete back that omits the noise-sensitive advisory obligation, and one near-duplicate activity that tests the same retrieval target as an existing question in a functionally identical format.

Paragraph 3-5-2 contains only two rules, and the items uniformly reflect them correctly. No factual errors were found. The primary quality defects are answer-length cues in three items and thin flashcard backs in two items. One directive_check activity tests verbatim recognition rather than understanding. Given the source is only two brief rules, cross-format coverage is reasonable and no items are genuinely harmful or unsafe.

Shard 45 covers paragraph 3-5-3 with two substantive source statements (one body rule, one note). The content is factually accurate across all items. However, the shard over-produces items for the available source material: 14 items for a single rule and a brief note creates duplication, with two questions and two activities testing the identical operational fact. Paragraph-location scaffolding appears in one question stem and one flashcard front, making paragraph numbering a retrieval cue instead of operational context. Two flashcard backs answer only 'No' without restating the rule, providing no retrieval value. One question and one activity frame the requirement as a singular detail when the source mandates both direction and velocity together, creating a misleading partial-requirement impression. The situation_action activity is a strength, embedding the rule in a realistic peer-pressure scenario. After remediation the shard retains 3 questions, 4 activities, and 4 flashcards.

This shard contains 20 items (6 questions, 6 activities, 8 flashcards) covering paragraph 3-6-1 on ASDE equipment usage, operational status determination, and the radar-only mode note. The source coverage is thorough — every substantive source statement has at least one learning item. Flashcards are uniformly well-formed, concise, and source-faithful. The primary defect pattern is thin explanations that restate the answer rather than teaching the controlling rule structure, and answer-length cues where the correct choice is visibly longer than the distractors. One question also has awkward grammar in the stem. No items are source-incorrect, educationally harmful, or redundant enough to warrant removal. Five items receive minor replacement interventions and one receives a major replacement for combined grammar and length-cue defects. Several automated negative_stem flags on true/false verification activities were false positives.

This shard contains 18 items covering paragraph 3-6-2 (ASDE target identification, false target verification, and temporary drop procedures). The items are generally source-correct and educationally coherent. One cross-format duplication exists between a question and an activity sharing identical stems and correct-answer concepts. Two items have thin explanations or backs that omit the full FAA form designation stated in the source. All automated negative_stem flags in this shard are false positives — none of the flagged items contain negative constructions. The situation_action activity (0154e3d4) is the strongest item, combining multiple 3-6-2 requirements into a realistic operational scenario with plausible distractors.

This shard contains 22 items covering paragraph 3-6-3 (INFORMATION USAGE): the six authorized uses of ASDE-derived information, the prohibition on routine exact-heading guidance, the directional taxi phraseology, pilot visual-navigation responsibility, and the unidentified-target runway-protection rule. Most items are source-correct and operationally sound. The phraseology_builder and situation_action activities are the strongest items in the shard. Five items require intervention: two activity removals for duplication or poor educational framing, one question and one activity with thin explanations that restate the answer rather than teach the principle, and one flashcard with a one-word back that omits the operational scope of the responsibility.

This shard covers paragraph 3-6-4 (Safety Logic Alert Responses) with strong source fidelity across all 19 items. Every operational concept from the source is represented: initial visual/ASDE assessment, airborne-before-threshold go-around, taxiway go-around, two-aircraft intersecting-runway alert, formation-flight exception, side-step/circle-to-land alternative, and catch-all judgment for other alerts. No factual errors, no paragraph-location trivia, and no manufactured phraseology were found.

Paragraph 3-7-1 (Ground Traffic Movement) is well-covered by this shard's 23 items, with strong source fidelity across all three formats. Every operational concept from the paragraph body and notes appears in at least one format, and most major concepts (basic movement approval rule, conditional/unconditional instruction prohibitions, 'cleared' prohibition, approved prefixes, 'full length' restriction, intersection-departure distances, and declared distances) have cross-format reinforcement via question + activity + flashcard combinations. The situation_action activity effectively composes multiple sub-rules into one scenario. No items ask paragraph-location trivia or manufacture unsourced phraseology errors. Two items need minor educational-quality interventions: one thin explanation and one answer-length cue.

All 16 items are operationally correct and sourced to 3-7-2. No items require removal or splitting. Six items need quality replacements: two thin explanations, three answer-length cues, and one paragraph-location scaffold. The paragraph covers taxi clearance content requirements, runway crossing procedures, hold-short readback rules, multiple-crossing conditions, and progressive taxi triggers — all well distributed across questions and activities with cross-format reinforcement between questions and knowledge_check / list_membership / situation_action activities.

This shard covers paragraph 3-7-2 with 11 flashcards and no questions or activities. Ten of the eleven flashcards are source-faithful, self-contained, and educationally coherent — each targets a specific operational rule, condition, procedure, or requirement using an appropriate card type. One flashcard (7721ac83) is unusable because its front references a taxi-route figure that does not exist in the source packet; the back substance is correct but stranded behind a paragraph-location scaffold. The shard is flashcard-heavy with zero retrieval diversity across question and activity formats. Several substantive source concepts remain uncovered, including vehicle runway clearance requirements, connecting taxiway omission rules, crossing-point orientation guidance, and LAHSO operations.

The shard covers paragraph 3-7-3 (Ground Operations — blast and wash hazard avoidance) with generally faithful source representation. Most items correctly reflect the two body directives (avoid excessive taxi power for super/heavy; avoid close helicopter proximity for small aircraft) and the note (use caution near larger aircraft; may use rotor wash, jet blast, or prop wash). Source fidelity is sound, but explanation quality is uneven: three items restate source text rather than teaching the controlling operational rule. One question uses vague paragraph-referencing language paired with distractor-length cues. One activity duplicates a question's concept and retrieval mode. One activity frames a list-recognition task as a single-approval question when three terms are authorized. The phraseology flashcards are appropriately scoped single-concept items despite thin backs. No items required removal for source error or educational harm.

The shard has strong source fidelity across all 16 items. Content is accurate to paragraph 3-7-4. The two weaknesses are answer-length cues in one question and one activity where the correct choice restates the full procedure in a single long option while distractors are terse. No items are dangerously wrong, educationally harmful, or format-inappropriate. Phraseology items and the spot-the-error activity are well-constructed. The situation-action activity provides genuine operational context.

This shard covers paragraph 3-7-5 (Precision Approach Critical Area) with faithful source content across 21 items. All eight flashcards are well-constructed single-retrieval-target cards covering triggers, exceptions, phraseology, and the DoD carve-out. The phraseology-builder and situation-action activities are strong. The two primary defects are answer-length cues in four questions and two activities where the correct multi-condition answer is dramatically longer than short generic distractors, and thin explanations in two questions and two activities that restate the answer instead of teaching the controlling rule. No items are source-wrong, educationally harmful, or format-inappropriate.

The shard covers paragraph 3-7-6 (POFZ and Final Approach OCS) across 21 items. All 8 flashcards are sound — concise, self-contained, and correctly keyed to source. Questions and activities exhibit three recurring defects: generic paragraph-location references (e.g. 'below the paragraph limits', 'below the 3-7-6 trigger') instead of operational weather criteria; answer-length cues where the correct choice is substantially longer than distractors; and explanations that restate the answer rather than teaching the controlling rule. No item is factually wrong or unsafe. 10 of 21 items required replacement to correct presentation-level defects.

All 19 items in this shard are correct, well-sourced from paragraph 3-8-1, and educationally coherent. No replacements, removals, or splits are required. The items demonstrate strong cross-format reinforcement — every source concept appears in at least two learning formats, which directly addresses the systemwide audit finding of weak cross-format coverage. The automated flag for answer_length_cue on question e0eb3c52 is inherent to the source body text structure and does not create a genuine answer-revealing cue given the plausible distractors. The thin_back flag on flashcard a23f0758 is a false positive; the two-word answer is the complete and correct retrieval target.

Shard covers a single, narrow paragraph (3-8-2) defining arrival-to-departure transition points for three maneuver types. Most items are factually correct and operationally sound, with good cross-maneuver distractors that exploit the other transition points. The dominant defects are paragraph-number references in stems and card fronts (3 items), thin explanations that omit the complete source rule (1 item), and cross-format verbatim duplication of questions as activities (2 activities removed). One true/false question was removed as redundant with an existing MCQ on the same fact.

All 19 items in this shard are source-accurate against paragraph 3-8-3 and TBL 3-8-1. No replacements, removals, or splits are required. The table-lookup items correctly reflect the four row/column value pairs; the condition-based items accurately capture conditions (a) and (b); the note-based items faithfully represent Note 1 (SRS categories) and Note 2 (wake turbulence). The situation_action activity is a strong operational-application item that forces the learner to identify the governing category (Heavy) and apply the correct centerline minimum against a plausible-but-wrong controller argument.

This shard covers paragraph 3-8-4 (simultaneous opposite-direction operations) with 19 items across three formats. Most items are factually correct and source-faithful. The main defects are document-scaffolding in three item stems, cross-format duplication between two question/activity pairs, and a few thin explanations that restate the answer rather than teaching the phraseology template or operational rule. The situation_action and phraseology_builder activities are well-constructed. Flashcard content is sound; the thin_back heuristic fires on several single-value flashcards where brevity is actually appropriate for the retrieval target.

Shard 61 covers paragraph 3-9-1 (Departure Information) with 17 items: 5 questions, 6 activities, and 6 flashcards. The shard is sound overall. Source fidelity is accurate across all items, distractors are plausible, difficulty levels are appropriate, and no item is factually wrong or educationally harmful. The primary defect is explanations that either reference the document structure ('the paragraph includes,' 'the paragraph explicitly states') instead of stating the operating requirement directly, or are thin restatements of the answer without teaching the controlling condition. Five of seventeen items required minor explanation rewrites. No items required removal or splitting.

Shard 62 covers takeoff clearance phraseology and conditions for runway number, intersection, full-length, shortened-runway, and close-proximity crossing clearances. The source facts are usable, but several items used paragraph references, thin explanations, or answer-length cues. Replacements preserve the source-bound operating requirements.

The shard covering paragraph 3-9-11 (Cancellation of Takeoff Clearance) demonstrates strong source coverage and factual fidelity across all three learning formats. All four essential source statements — the pre-roll cancellation rule, the post-roll safety-only limitation, the EDCT/TM prohibition, and the approved phraseology — are represented in questions, flashcards, and activities. No item teaches incorrect information or contains unsafe guidance. However, explanation quality is a consistent weakness: 8 of 17 items have explanations that either restate the answer without teaching the controlling principle or rely on document-structure scaffolding rather than stating the operating requirement directly. One flashcard back reduces to a single word, failing to serve as a self-contained retrieval target. The scenario-based activity (situation_action) and phraseology activities (builder, spot-the-error) are well-constructed and educationally effective.

This shard covers a single paragraph (3-9-2, Departure Delay Information) with four operational sub-rules and four phraseology blocks. The items broadly cover the source content — gate-hold advisory, engine-start advisories, delay-area approval, and gate-hold termination notification. Source fidelity is mostly sound, but the full gate-hold advisory phraseology is systematically truncated across three formats (question, activity, flashcard), each omitting the EXPECT ENGINE START/TAXI (time) sentence. Several explanations lean on document-structure scaffolding rather than stating the operating requirement directly. One question stem uses permissive language ('may') where the source mandates ('must'). The situation_action and phraseology_builder activities are well-constructed with appropriate operational context.

This shard covering 3-9-3 is source-faithful and operationally sound. All items accurately reflect the departure control instruction requirements, and cross-format coverage of the three core concepts (SID frequency exception, pre-takeoff departure control information, and the military 2,500-foot altitude restriction) is strong. The primary defect is explanation quality: nearly half the items use document-location scaffolding or thin restatements instead of teaching the operating requirement directly. One flashcard front sacrifices specificity with a vague qualifier.

The shard covers core LUAW operational rules from paragraph 3-9-4 with adequate factual accuracy. No items contain source fidelity errors or unsafe operational guidance. The primary defects are answer-length cues in three items where the correct choice is markedly longer than distractors, document-location scaffolding in two explanations ('The note says,' 'The paragraph places'), a thin explanation that merely echoes the target phrase, and one flashcard with an incomplete back that hand-waves at other conditions with 'among the other listed conditions.'

This shard covers a single paragraph (3-9-5) containing one substantive sentence about anticipating separation for takeoff. All items accurately reflect the source material. However, the 12-item allocation for one sentence produces significant repetition — four questions, four activities, and four flashcards all testing the same rule through different angles. The most notable defects are paragraph-location explanations in two items, an answer-length cue in one multiple-choice question, a flashcard whose back is a single word ('No'), and context-free flashcard backs that do not support effective retrieval.

Moderate. All 16 items are factually accurate with no source-fidelity errors, and activity types are well-matched to the skills tested. However, paragraph-location scaffolding and thin explanations degrade educational value across a majority of items. Ten of sixteen items require replacement to remove document-structure references or to strengthen explanations.

The shard contains 10 flashcards covering paragraph 3-9-6 (Same Runway Separation). No questions or activities present. Overall quality is adequate: seven of ten cards are sound as-is, including all four minima retrieval cards, the phraseology card, the high-level procedure summary card, and the wake turbulence hold card. Three cards need replacement for paragraph-location scaffolding, thin backs, and overgeneralized question scope.

The shard is factually accurate with no source-fidelity errors. All 22 items map correctly to the wake turbulence intersection departure rules in paragraph 3-9-7. The primary defect pattern is paragraph-location scaffolding in explanations and answer text, affecting 6 items where phrases like 'the note says,' 'the paragraph specifically says,' 'in 3-9-7,' and 'the listed cases' substitute document-structure references for direct statements of the operating requirement. A secondary pattern is thin explanations that merely restate the correct answer rather than teaching when and why the rule applies, affecting 2 items. Two flashcard fronts rely on anaphoric references ('the same intersection-departure cases') that leave the card context-light when studied in isolation. One flashcard back is too thin ('No') to support reverse retrieval. The situation-action activity (b36c1c9c) is well-constructed and tests operational decision-making effectively, but the correct answer text embeds paragraph-location scaffolding.

This shard covers wake turbulence intervals for intersecting runways, arrival-protection conditions for departure release, the LAHSO alternate protection, the traffic-information requirement, and the prohibition on pilot-requested deviation from required intervals. The items are factually accurate against the source blocks and the distractors represent plausible operational mistakes. The primary defect is document-location scaffolding in explanations (phrases like 'the paragraph says,' 'the listed conditions,' 'in 3-9-8') across seven items. One flashcard has a front/back number mismatch. No items are wrong, redundant, or educationally harmful in substance.

Shard 72 covers paragraph 3-9-9 (Nonintersecting Converging Runway Operations) with 17 items across three formats. The core operational content — separation protections for converging-runway departures, the 1 NM extended-centerline crossover threshold, and wake turbulence intervals — is represented, but paragraph-location scaffolding and thin, answer-restating explanations are pervasive across questions, activities, and flashcards.

This shard covering paragraph 3-10-1 (Landing Information) is well-constructed. Source fidelity is high across all 19 items. The core operational distinctions — ATIS code authorizing omission of ATIS landing information, 'have numbers' authorizing omission of only runway/wind/altimeter, 'have numbers' not indicating ATIS receipt, and the shortened-runway announcement requirement — are tested across multiple formats with plausible distractors. The situation_action activity effectively combines two related rules. Two minor interventions are needed: one question explanation uses prohibited document-structure scaffolding, and one flashcard has a front/back mismatch.

The shard covers paragraph 3-10-10 (Altitude Restricted Low Approach) with 19 items across questions, activities, and flashcards. Source fidelity is strong — all correct answers faithfully reflect the paragraph's operational content. Distractors are generally plausible and well-constructed. The phraseology builder and situation_action activities are standouts: the builder tests exact prescribed phraseology with a realistic word bank, and the situation_action presents a complex multi-constraint scenario that requires synthesizing the personnel restriction, holding-aircraft prohibition, and traffic advisory duty. However, eight items carry document-structure scaffolding that substitutes paragraph-number or document-format trivia for operational framing. Two items also have answer-length cues or document-trivia framing that undercuts educational coherence.

Shard covers paragraph 3-10-11 (CLOSED TRAFFIC) with 18 items across three formats. Content is generally source-faithful and operationally grounded, but five items carry document-structure scaffolding ("the paragraph," "the note," paragraph numbers) in stems or explanations, one activity is a near-exact duplicate of a question, and two items have explanations too thin to teach the operating principle.

This shard covers paragraph 3-10-12 (Overhead Maneuver) with 5 questions, 8 activities, and 7 flashcards. All 20 items are source-faithful with no factual errors. The content is generally well-constructed: distractors represent plausible operational mistakes, cross-format reinforcement exists for key concepts (IFR cancellation point, pattern altitude omission, conflict prevention), and phraseology items preserve exact recall where appropriate. Seven items need minor intervention — three for answer-length cues and four for explanation quality issues involving document-structure scaffolding or figure-label references.

The shard is accurate and source-faithful. All answers are correct and all distractors are operationally plausible. The primary defects are systematic: four multiple-choice questions carry answer-length cues where the correct answer is conspicuously longer than distractors; three activities have explanations that are either too thin or lean on document-structure scaffolding instead of stating the operating requirement directly. No items are wrong, redundant, or harmful. No removals or splits are needed.

The shard is accurate and source-faithful. All correct answers match the source blocks and no item contains a factual error. The primary defects are systematic: three multiple-choice questions carry answer-length cues where the correct choice is conspicuously longer than distractors; two items use paragraph-location scaffolding in the stem ('in this paragraph', 'under this paragraph'); one activity explanation leans on document-structure language; and two activities are near-duplicates of questions testing the same forwarding-list entries in the same retrieval mode. No items are operationally harmful or require a blocker-level intervention.

The shard covers paragraph 3-10-3 (Same Runway Separation) with 22 items that are factually accurate and well-aligned with the source. Items test operational minima recall, rule application for arriving aircraft behind landed or departed traffic, category-based distance exceptions, visual separation for helicopters and powered-lift, and wake turbulence advisory requirements. The scenario-based activities and figure-interpretation items are particular strengths. The primary defect is thin explanations that restate the answer instead of teaching the controlling operational rule; this affects six items. Paragraph-location scaffolding appears in two items. One question has a moderate answer-length imbalance.

This shard covers paragraph 3-10-4 (intersecting runway operations, LAHSO, and crossing-runway wake turbulence) across 21 items — 6 questions, 7 activities, and 8 flashcards. Source fidelity is generally high, with most items correctly reflecting the operative requirements. The primary defects are document-location scaffolding in stems and card fronts ("under this paragraph," "subparagraphs b1 and b2," "the listed cases"), one underspecified activity whose directive statement uses an undefined placeholder, one thin question explanation that merely restates the answer instead of teaching the rule, and one activity explanation that omits the weight-restriction basis for the correct answer. No items require removal or splitting. After nine targeted replacements the shard will be coherent and self-contained.

The shard is high quality. All 21 items are factually accurate against the paragraph 3-10-5 source blocks. Most explanations state operational requirements directly without document-structure scaffolding. Two interventions needed: one near-duplicate activity removal and one explanation improvement on a phraseology builder.

The shard covers paragraph 3-10-6 with reasonably strong source fidelity. Most items correctly capture the anticipation rule, traffic-information triggers, LUAW prohibition with its exception, and the prescribed phraseology. The primary defect is document-structure scaffolding — several stems and explanations reference 'the note,' 'this paragraph,' 'the example phraseology,' or 'the paragraph' instead of stating the operating requirement directly. One pair of flashcards is a near-duplicate testing the same retrieval target. No safety-critical errors were found; all correct answers are factually accurate to the source blocks.

This shard covers a single, narrow paragraph (3-10-7, Landing Clearance Without Visual Observation) with a concise rule, one phraseology, and one note. Source fidelity is generally correct across all items. Several items are well-constructed, particularly the phraseology_builder (f6de6460), spot_the_error (2b173c45), and situation_action (b5436d00) activities. However, the shard carries significant redundancy — the same core rule is tested across eight items in three entity types, with multiple items overlapping nearly identically. Three items contain document-structure references ('in this paragraph,' 'the note states'), and two items test word-order trivia rather than operational knowledge. One question has a pronounced answer-length cue.

This shard covers a single, concise paragraph (3-10-8) with only three operational elements. Paragraph 3-10-9 is a procedureally dense source covering runway-exit instructions, taxi-instruction triggers, clearance expectations, intersection protection, and readback requirements. The shard's items are source-faithful with no factual errors detected. The primary defects are thin explanations that restate answers instead of teaching the operational rule, answer-length cues in dual-condition items, and one flashcard that teaches a concrete example instead of the general phraseology template. No item was wrong enough to warrant removal, and no overloaded items required splitting. Cross-format reinforcement is strong for the timing prohibition and the dual-condition taxi rule, though the phraseology card needs correction.

This shard contains 22 items covering helicopter surface taxi, hover-taxi, and air-taxi operations from paragraph 3-11-1. Overall quality is high: concepts are covered across all three formats, distractors are operationally plausible, explanations mostly state controlling rules directly, and the scenario-based activity integrates multiple sub-paragraph requirements into a realistic operational decision. Two questions require minor intervention — one for document-structure scaffolding in its explanation and one for distractor length imbalance — and one activity duplicates a question in the same retrieval mode. The remaining 19 items are correct, self-contained, and educationally coherent. Three flashcards were flagged for thin backs but each tests recall of a single defined term or name, which is appropriate for the retrieval target. Two activities were flagged for negative stems but neither contains one; both flags are false positives.

The shard covers a single paragraph (3-11-2, helicopter/powered-lift takeoff clearance) and is generally well constructed. Source fidelity is high across all formats. The core operational rules — preference for takeoff clearance over extended hover-taxi, the own-risk departure alternative for non-movement/unauthorized/off-airport locations, the 5-knot tailwind limit with pilot-agreement exception, and the constituent phraseology elements — are each represented across multiple formats. The spot-the-error, phraseology-builder, and situation-action activities provide good retrieval diversity. No item is factually wrong or educationally harmful.

The shard covers paragraph 3-11-3, which defines two departure release conditions and one definitional note. The recurring issues are thin explanations that restate the answer instead of teaching the operational requirement, generic references to 'this rule' that prevent stems from being self-contained, paragraph-location scaffolding in two items, and one activity type mismatch.

This shard covers a single paragraph (3-11-4) with two operational conditions. This shard covers a single short paragraph (3-11-5) with one source block establishing two operational requirements for simultaneous helicopter or powered-lift landings or takeoffs: at least 200 feet between points, nonconflicting courses, and the two methods to determine spacing (surface markings or instruct the aircraft to remain at least 200 feet apart). The 16 items provide thorough cross-format coverage of these concepts across questions, activities, and flashcards. No items are factually wrong or educationally harmful. Shard quality is high. This shard covers paragraph 3-12-1, which contains a single sentence defining the application scope of sea-lane provisions. Paragraph-location scaffolding is the dominant defect across questions, activities, and flashcards. The one situation-action activity is the strongest item; most others need either scaffolding removal or outright removal to eliminate duplicates.

This shard covers sea lane departure separation rules (3-12-2) with good cross-format coverage of key operational requirements: normal release conditions behind preceding departures and arrivals, the landmark-distance exception with category-based minima, and floatplane caution procedures. Chapter 3 paragraph 3-12-3 sea lane arrival separation content is substantially accurate and draws faithfully from the source. operations.problem: The stem opens with 'Under the note to 3-1-1' — paragraph-location scaffolding that tests document navigation instead of the operational rule that the pilot retains collision-avoidance responsibility. The distractor 'shared legal duty created by the note' also embeds a document-structure reference.
operations.source_basis: Note block: 'When operating in accordance with CFRs, it is the responsibility of the pilot to avoid collision with other aircraft.'

match.question_text: Under the note to 3-1-1, who remains responsible for avoiding collision when operating in accordance with the CFRs?

replacements.question_text: Even when ATC provides traffic information near terminal locations, who remains responsible for collision avoidance under the CFRs?
replacements.explanation: When operating in accordance with CFRs, the pilot remains responsible for avoiding collision with other aircraft. ATC-provided traffic information can aid pilots near terminal locations but does not transfer or share that responsibility.

choices.text: The pilot.
The local controller exclusively.
The pilot and controller equally under a shared legal duty.
The airport manager whenever the aircraft is inside the surface area.

operations.problem: The correct answer is substantially longer than every distractor and is the only choice structured as a multi-element list, making it guessable on form alone. The stem also references 'the note' as a document artifact rather than asking about the operational scope directly.
operations.source_basis: Note block: 'traffic information can aid pilots in avoiding collision between aircraft operating within Class B, Class C, or Class D surface areas and the terminal radar service areas, and transiting aircraft operating in proximity to terminal locations.'

match.question_text: Where does the note say traffic information can aid pilots in avoiding collision?

replacements.question_text: In which areas does traffic information especially aid pilots in avoiding collisions?
replacements.explanation: Due to the limited space around terminal locations, traffic information can aid pilots in avoiding collisions among aircraft operating within Class B, C, or D surface areas, terminal radar service areas, and transiting aircraft near terminal locations.

choices.text: Within Class B, C, or D surface areas, terminal radar service areas, and for transiting aircraft near terminal locations.
Within Class B and Class C surface areas, and for transiting aircraft near terminal locations.
Within Class B, C, or D surface areas and for transiting aircraft near terminal locations.
Within terminal radar service areas and for transiting aircraft operating near terminal locations.

operations.problem: The explanation reads 'That is the controlling directive in 3-1-1' — a paragraph-location reference that substitutes for teaching the operational principle. It is also thin: it offers no guidance on what the rule means in practice.
operations.source_basis: Body block: 'Provide airport traffic control service based only upon observed or known traffic and airport conditions.'

content.question_text: Provide airport traffic control service based only on observed or known traffic and airport conditions.

choices.text: True
False

content.explanation: That is the controlling directive in 3-1-1.

content.question_text: Provide airport traffic control service based only on observed or known traffic and airport conditions.

choices.text: True
False

content.explanation: Controllers must base airport traffic control decisions only on traffic and airport conditions they actually observe or know — not on assumptions, guesses, or expectations about what is probably happening.

operations.problem: The front asks 'What does 3-1-1 exclude as a basis for service?' — the paragraph-number reference turns this into a document-trivia question instead of testing recall of the operational exclusion.
operations.source_basis: Body block: 'Provide airport traffic control service based only upon observed or known traffic and airport conditions.' The logical contrapositive excludes assumptions, guesses, and unknown conditions.

match.front: What does 3-1-1 exclude as a basis for service?
match.back: Assumptions, guesses, or unknown traffic or airport conditions.

replacements.front: What is excluded as a basis for providing airport traffic control service?
replacements.back: Assumptions, guesses, or unknown traffic or airport conditions.

operations.problem: Fill-in-the-blank item has only one choice ('UP'), making it trivially answerable with no distractors and no assessment value. The stem asks the user to complete 'LANDING GEAR APPEARS _____' which tests rote recall of a specific printed example rather than the operational skill of recognizing the standard phraseology format.
operations.source_basis: The phraseology block states the format: '(Item) APPEAR/S (observed condition).' The body block requires the controller to inform an aircraft of any observed abnormal condition. The operational skill is knowing and applying the standard format, not recalling which specific examples appear in the source.

match.question_text: Complete the advisory: "LANDING GEAR APPEARS _____"

replacements.question_text: What is the standard phraseology format for informing a pilot of an observed abnormal aircraft condition?
replacements.explanation: The standard format is '(Item) APPEAR/S (observed condition).' The item names what appears abnormal and the observed condition describes its state. The listed examples such as 'Landing gear appears up' and 'Rear baggage door appears open' both follow this format.

choices.text: (Item) APPEAR/S (observed condition).
(Item) IS (observed condition).
OBSERVE (condition) ON (item).
(Observed condition) ON (item) NOTED.

operations.problem: The stem references paragraph 3-1-10 by number, testing document-navigation trivia instead of applying the phraseology rule. The original explanation restates the format without connecting it to the operational context.
operations.source_basis: The phraseology block defines the format '(Item) APPEAR/S (observed condition).' The listed examples illustrate correct usage: 'Landing gear appears up,' 'Landing gear appears down and in place,' and 'Rear baggage door appears open.'

match.question_text: Which of the following best matches the abnormality-advisory phraseology in 3-1-10?

replacements.question_text: Which of the following best matches the abnormality-advisory phraseology?
replacements.explanation: The abnormality-advisory phraseology uses the format '(Item) APPEAR/S (observed condition).' The correct choice follows this format. The other choices use structures and vocabulary that do not match the prescribed phraseology.

choices.text: Landing gear appears down and in place.
I think your landing gear is probably down, verify when able.
Possible baggage issue observed, use caution at your discretion.
Suspected abnormal condition, check all systems and advise.

operations.problem: The card front references paragraph 3-1-10 by number, testing document-navigation trivia rather than recall of the phraseology format. The back content and card type are correct.
operations.source_basis: The phraseology block defines the format: '(Item) APPEAR/S (observed condition).'

match.front: What is the basic abnormality-advisory format in 3-1-10?
match.back: "(Item) APPEAR/S (observed condition)."

replacements.front: What is the basic abnormality-advisory format?
replacements.back: "(Item) APPEAR/S (observed condition)."

operations.problem: The correct answer is substantially longer than all three distractors, creating a structural answer-length cue that allows guessing without operational knowledge. The explanation restates the answer instead of teaching the controlling rule.
operations.source_basis: 3-1-11a states controllers may approve a pilot's request to cross Class C or D surface areas or exceed the speed limit when traffic permits, but not above 250 knots unless the pilot reports a higher minimum speed is required.

match.question_text: If traffic conditions permit, what may a controller approve in Class C or Class D surface areas?

replacements.question_text: If traffic conditions permit, what may a controller approve in Class C or Class D surface areas?
replacements.explanation: Controllers may approve a pilot's request to cross Class C or Class D surface areas or exceed the speed limit when traffic conditions permit, but speeds above 250 knots may be approved only when the pilot reports that a higher minimum speed is necessary.

choices.text: A request to cross the surface area or to exceed the speed limit, within the 250-knot cap.
Any unusual maneuver the pilot wishes to perform, including low passes and flybys.
Any speed the pilot requests, with no conditional restriction on the speed limit.
A buzz job, provided it is conducted briefly and does not generate noise complaints.

operations.problem: The stem asks about 'the paragraph's unusual-maneuver exception' — a generic document reference that teaches paragraph-location thinking instead of operational recognition. The correct answer is also significantly longer than the distractors, creating an answer-length cue.
operations.source_basis: The 3-1-11 exception permits unusual maneuvers only when conducted under a letter of agreement and without adverse effect on safety or reduction of service to other users.

match.question_text: When does the paragraph's unusual-maneuver exception apply?

replacements.question_text: Under what conditions may unusual maneuvers be approved in Class B, C, or D surface areas?
replacements.explanation: Unusual maneuvers in Class B, C, or D surface areas may be approved only when conducted under a letter of agreement and without adverse effect on the safety of the air traffic operation or any reduction in service to other users.

choices.text: When conducted under a letter of agreement and having no adverse safety effect or service reduction.
When the pilot requests the maneuver in advance and promises to remain in visual conditions.
When the maneuver is conducted at or below 250 knots indicated airspeed throughout.
When the airport arrival rate is below normal and the controller has available capacity.

operations.problem: The stem asks which item 'is identified in the note as' an unusual maneuver, framing the question around document structure rather than operational classification of maneuvers. The distractors and correct answer are otherwise well-constructed.
operations.source_basis: The 3-1-11 note lists unnecessary low passes, unscheduled flybys, practice instrument approaches below specified minima, and buzz jobs as unusual maneuvers that increase hazards to persons and property and contribute to noise complaints.

match.question_text: Which of the following is identified in the note as an unusual maneuver that increases hazards and noise complaints?

replacements.question_text: Which of the following is considered an unusual maneuver that increases hazards to persons and property and contributes to noise complaints?
replacements.explanation: Unusual maneuvers include unnecessary low passes, unscheduled flybys, practice instrument approaches to altitudes below specified minima unless a landing or touch-and-go is to be made, and buzz jobs conducted at low altitude or high speed for thrill purposes. These maneuvers increase hazards to persons and property and contribute to noise complaints.

choices.text: A so-called buzz job conducted at low altitude or high speed for thrill purposes.
A normal touch-and-go conducted under tower instruction.
A routine transition through Class D at authorized speed.
A runway crossing taxi clearance by airport operations.

operations.problem: This knowledge_check activity tests the same concept as question 8d22d7fc — what a controller may approve in Class C or D surface areas when traffic permits — using the same multiple-choice recognition format. The duplication wastes learner time without adding retrieval diversity or a different cognitive demand. The concept remains tested by the question.
operations.source_basis: The core rule from 3-1-11a about approving surface-area crossings and speed-limit exceptions is already tested by question 8d22d7fc in the same multiple-choice recognition format.

content.question_text: If traffic conditions permit, what may you approve in Class C or Class D surface areas?

choices.text: A pilot's request to cross the surface area or exceed the surface-area speed limit, subject to the 250-knot cap unless the pilot reports a higher required minimum speed.
Any unusual maneuver the pilot wants to perform as long as the pilot remains in two-way communications.
Any speed the pilot requests, including well above 250 knots, without further conditions.
A buzz job if it shortens the arrival path and reduces pattern congestion.

content.explanation: Crossing the surface area or exceeding the normal speed limit may be approved if traffic permits, but the 250-knot cap and higher-minimum-speed exception still apply.

operations.problem: This requirement_check activity tests the same true-false statement as question 14b5683e — that a speed above 250 knots may be approved only if the pilot reports a higher minimum speed is required. choices.text: True
False
